questions.questionText: Acute appendicitis classically presents with pain that migrates from:
questions.optionB: Periumbilical region to right iliac fossa (McBurney's point) — due to visceral → parietal peritoneal irritation
questions.explanation: Acute appendicitis: most common surgical emergency. Migration of pain: initially periumbilical (visceral — T10 dermatome, midgut referred pain) → localizes to RIF at McBurney's point (parietal peritoneum involved). McBurney's point: junction of lateral 1/3 and medial 2/3 of line from ASIS to umbilicus. Signs: Rovsing (RIF pain on LIF pressure), psoas sign (retrocecal appendix), obturator sign (pelvic appendix). Diagnosis: clinical + Alvarado score ± CT/USG. Treatment: appendectomy. Reference: Bailey & Love — Appendicitis.

questions.questionText: The most common type of intestinal obstruction in adults is:
questions.optionB: Adhesions from previous surgery (most common cause of small bowel obstruction in developed countries)
questions.explanation: Small bowel obstruction causes: Adhesions (#1 in adults — post-surgical), hernias (#2, #1 in virgin abdomen), tumors, Crohn's disease. Large bowel obstruction: carcinoma (#1), volvulus (#2, sigmoid > cecal), diverticular disease. Presentation: colicky abdominal pain, vomiting (early in proximal SBO, late in LBO), absolute constipation (complete obstruction), abdominal distension. XR: dilated loops, air-fluid levels, absence of distal gas. Management: drip and suck (NGT decompression + IVF) → surgery if strangulation/failed conservative. Reference: Bailey & Love — Intestinal Obstruction.

questions.questionText: Inguinal hernia that passes through the deep inguinal ring, inguinal canal, and superficial ring is classified as:
questions.optionB: Indirect inguinal hernia (follows the path of the spermatic cord — most common type of groin hernia)
questions.explanation: Indirect inguinal hernia: protrudes through DEEP (internal) inguinal ring → traverses inguinal canal → exits through superficial (external) ring. Most common groin hernia overall (both sexes, all ages). Follows processus vaginalis (congenital). Lateral to inferior epigastric artery. Direct: medial to inferior epigastric a., through Hesselbach triangle (weak transversalis fascia), older adults. Femoral: below inguinal ligament through femoral canal, more common in women, highest risk of strangulation. Treatment: mesh repair (Lichtenstein — open, or laparoscopic TEP/TAPP). Reference: Bailey & Love — Inguinal Hernia.

questions.optionB: Sudden severe epigastric pain, board-like rigidity, and air under diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on erect CXR
questions.explanation: Peptic ulcer perforation: duodenal (most common) > gastric. Anterior wall perforations → peritonitis. Presentation: sudden, severe 'knife-like' epigastric pain, board-like rigidity (generalized guarding), rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds (paralytic ileus). Erect CXR: free gas under diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum — present in ~75%). Lateral decubitus XR if patient cannot sit. Treatment: EMERGENCY surgery — Graham patch (omental patch) closure ± definitive ulcer surgery. Laparoscopic repair increasingly common. Reference: Bailey & Love — Peptic Perforation.

questions.questionText: Colorectal cancer screening for average-risk individuals should begin at age:
questions.optionB: 45-50 years (colonoscopy every 10 years or FOBT/FIT annually)
questions.explanation: CRC screening: average risk → begin at 45-50 years (ACS: 45, USPSTF: 45). Methods: colonoscopy (gold standard, every 10 years), FOBT/FIT (annual), flexible sigmoidoscopy (every 5 years), CT colonography (every 5 years). High-risk (FAP, Lynch/HNPCC, IBD, family history): earlier screening. Left-sided CRC: obstruction, altered bowel habits, pencil-thin stools. Right-sided: anemia, occult bleeding, mass. Duke's staging: A (mucosa/submucosa), B (through muscularis), C (lymph nodes+), D (distant mets). Reference: Bailey & Love — CRC Screening.
